changesets/changesets#1762.body:
Hi maintainers 👋 ### Summary A recently-flagged security advisory affects the `js-yaml` dependency used transitively within several `@changesets/*` packages. All `js-yaml` versions below `4.1.0` are affected: [Prototype pollution in merge](https://github.com/nodeca/js-yaml/security/advisories/[REDACTED_GHSA]) The dependency chain (via `@changesets/cli@2.29.7`) looks like this: ``` @changesets/cli@2.29.7 |-- @changesets/read@0.6.5 |-- @changesets/parse@0.4.1 |-- js-yaml@3.14.1 |-- @manypkg/get-packages@1.1.3 |-- read-yaml-file@1.1.0 |-- js-yaml@3.14.1 (deduped) ``` ### Attempted Mitigation I've attempted to override `js-yaml` in our project to the patched version (4.1.1): ```jsonc "overrides": { "js-yaml": "4.1.1" }, ``` …but this results in runtime failures when executing `changeset version`:
Error Output ```less npx changeset version 🦋 error Error: could not parse changeset - invalid frontmatter: --- 🦋 error at Object.parseChangesetFile [as default] (.../node_modules/@changesets/parse/dist/changesets-parse.cjs.js:35:11) 🦋 error at .../node_modules/@changesets/read/dist/changesets-read.cjs.js:148:71 🦋 error at async Promise.all (index 0) 🦋 error at async Object.getChangesets [as default] (.../node_modules/@changesets/read/dist/changesets-read.cjs.js:152:48) 🦋 error at async version (.../node_modules/@changesets/cli/dist/changesets-cli.cjs.js:1155:34) 🦋 error at async run (.../node_modules/@changesets/cli/dist/changesets-cli.cjs.js:1339:11) ```
It appears that `@changesets/parse` depends on YAML parsing behaviour that changed in `js-yaml@4.x`, making the override incompatible. ### Environment - `@changesets/cli`: 2.29.7 - Node.js: 22.19.0 ### Request Would it be possible to update the relevant `@changesets/*` packages to use `js-yaml@^4.1.1`, or otherwise confirm whether upgrading is feasible? Given this is a security advisory (prototype pollution in merge), having a path to a secure version would be very helpful for downstream consumers. Thanks for maintaining this project 😄
changesets/changesets#1762.title: Security Advisory: `js-yaml` Prototype Pollution (CVE) used via `@changesets/*` Dependencies

jestjs/jest#9885.body:
## 🐛 Bug Report

```js
import t from './file';
import d from './directory/'
```

These should both fail to resolve, but since we use the same resolver as for CJS, it doesn't, and the import will work while it would have failed at runtime. This is also the case for dynamic imports.

This might need some support in [`resolve`](https://github.com/browserify/resolve) whenever they add support for ESM? https://github.com/browserify/resolve/issues/222

Place where we call resolve in jest: https://github.com/facebook/jest/blob/c024dec130d9914dcc3418ea74c26f667db3dbfa/packages/jest-runtime/src/index.ts#L395-L398
https://github.com/facebook/jest/blob/c024dec130d9914dcc3418ea74c26f667db3dbfa/packages/jest-runtime/src/index.ts#L423

Node docs: https://nodejs.org/api/esm.html#esm_mandatory_file_extensions
Node issue for better errors: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/30603
jestjs/jest#9885.title: ESM should require file extensions

pnpm/pnpm#2008.body:
## Problem

One of the advantages of format such as YAML and JSON5 is that it supports comments. Commands that modifies these files render these comments pointless.

For example, this `package.yaml`:

```yaml
dependencies:

  # the following dependencies are required to do something
  foo: 0.1.2
  bar: 1.2.3

  # the following dependencies are others' peer
  abc: 2.3.4 # peer dependency of foo
  def: 3.4.5 # peer dependency of bar
```

will be turn into this:

```yaml
dependencies:
  abc: 2.3.4
  bar: 1.2.3
  def: 3.4.5
  foo: 0.1.2
```

## Suggestion

### 1. Attempt on a subset of YAML first

YAML is a complicated format, as such, it is understandable to use libraries such as `js-yaml` to write a YAML files. However, most of the time, YAML that users write is rather simple: No leading indent before `dependencies` and `devDependencies` field, one level of indent before each dependency name. For this reason, one can remove/insert lines with correct indentation in between YAML content and get the desired result.

**Possible strategy:**

1. Check if the YAML string satisfies a strict subset (such as No JSON block).
2. If it satisfies, treat YAML like a list of lines.
3. If it doesn't, and if user allows it, use YAML library to deserialize object.

### 2. Do not edit YAML file by default

pnpm may choose to ignore `package.yaml` file and print a warning, or pnpm may emit an error, depends on the command.

### 3. New command-line flags

When YAML file satisfies the subset
* `pnpm install --append-yaml-lines`: Add new lines to the end of dependencies/devDependencies list.
* `pnpm install --prepend-yaml-lines`: Add new lines to the beginning of dependencies/devDependencies list.
* `pnpm install --under-yaml-comment [number|string]`: Add new lines to under a comment block.

When YAML file does not satisfy the subset:
* `pnpm <command> --[no-]force-dump-yaml`: Whether to use YAML library to dump YAML content.

## This may also apply to JSON5

JSON5 has the following features: Comments, trailing commas, etc.
pnpm/pnpm#2008.title: Request: Preserve comments and key orders in `package.yaml`/`pnpm-workspace.yaml` when run 'pnpm install --save', 'pnpm update --latest', etc.

pnpm/pnpm#3816.body:
- [x] I'd be willing to implement this feature ([contributing guide](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md))

**Describe the user story**

Some alternate publishing flows would like to be able to customize `package.json` contents at publish time more than `pnpm` currently does.
Examples:
- The version field may need to be overwritten because the metadata about currently publishing version is stored outside of `package.json` (e.g. in a changelog file). This would also necessarily imply that rewriting of `workspace:` specifiers would also need to be overridden.
- The developer may which to delete fields that are only used for dev-time metadata (e.g. jest config settings, entries in the "scripts" field that are only for local builds, the entire "devDependencies" section to reduce size)

**Describe the solution you'd like**
I would request adding a `readPackageForPublishing` hook to `.pnpmfile.cjs` analogous to the existing `readPackage` hook, except invoked by `pnpm pack` as part of rewriting `package.json` to pack the tarball instead of during `pnpm install` or `pnpm update`.
This allows any customization to piggy-back on the existing rewriting of `package.json` that is performed by `pnpm` to rewrite `workspace:` specifiers, update license information, etc.

**Describe the drawbacks of your solution**
This will add an extra dependency to `pnpm pack` in the form of the pnpmfile, if defined.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
The existing approach is to locally modify the file, then invoke `pnpm pack`, then revert the file, which is how `pnpm` itself previously operated, calling `npm pack` internally. It works, but it involves modifying the source files of the package on disk, which makes the operation unsafe to perform concurrently with other processes that may be interested in reading `package.json`. It also makes it easy to end up in a bad state if an error occurs during packing or the process is terminated.
pnpm/pnpm#3816.title: readPackageForPublishing pnpmfile hook

pnpm/pnpm#6100.body:
- [ ] I'd be willing to implement this feature ([contributing guide](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md))

**Describe the user story**

As a developer, I need a command like `pnpm ci` to use when it's suggested to use `npm ci`

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Would be nice if we had in PNPM a CLI option like [NPM's `ci`](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v9/commands/npm-ci)
[I found other people running into the same issue](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/70154568/pnpm-equivalent-command-for-npm-ci).

**Describe the drawbacks of your solution**

If someone has a `ci` script, so the proposed feature will change the behavior of `pnpm ci` command.
But once PNPM is meant to stay close to the NPM API, it should have the same commands.
People who use a `ci` script still can use `pnpm run ci`, and the API becomes more close to NPM, facilitating migrating from one to another.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

In Unix-like environments, it appears that people can use `CI=true pnpm i` to accomplish the same behavior.
One can even define a `ci` script with this command.
But It's not a cross-platform solution and don't looks a good solution to recommend end-users to use.
Besides needing to introduce this script manually in every project you need, or manually in the command line every time.
pnpm/pnpm#6100.title: Implement NPM's `ci` command

### pnpm version: 8.6.5

### Code to reproduce the issue:

We have a custom NPM registry running on Artifactory. The URL of this is of the form `https://artifactory:443` with an explicit port number.

`.npmrc`:

```
registry=https://artifactory:443/artifactory/api/npm/npm-virtual/

auto-install-peers=false
lockfile-include-tarball-url=true
resolution-mode=highest
strict-peer-dependencies=true
```

Run `pnpm i uuid`:

```
  /uuid@9.0.0:
    resolution: {integrity: sha512-MXcSTerfPa4uqyzStbRoTgt5XIe3x5+42+q1sDuy3R5MDk66URdLMOZe5aPX/SQd+kuYAh0FdP/pO28IkQyTeg==, tarball: https://artifactory:443/artifactory/api/npm/npm-virtual/uuid/-/uuid-9.0.0.tgz}
    hasBin: true
    dev: false
```

Remove `pnpm-lock.yaml` and run `pnpm i`:

```
  /uuid@9.0.0:
    resolution: {integrity: sha512-MXcSTerfPa4uqyzStbRoTgt5XIe3x5+42+q1sDuy3R5MDk66URdLMOZe5aPX/SQd+kuYAh0FdP/pO28IkQyTeg==, tarball: https://artifactory/artifactory/api/npm/npm-virtual/uuid/-/uuid-9.0.0.tgz}
    hasBin: true
    dev: false
```

(Note, the port number has disappeared)

### Expected behavior:

`tarball` values should stable and have the same values independent of how the package is resolved.

### Actual behavior:

Sometimes the `tarball` has the port number `:443` and sometimes it does not. For example:
- On a fresh install, it does
- When the package existed in node_modules but not `pnpm-lock.yaml`, it does not.

This makes PRs from Renovate very large as tarballs change even when packages do not.

### Additional information:

 - `node -v` prints: v16.17.1
 - Windows, macOS, or Linux?: Linux
pnpm/pnpm#6725.title: tarball URLs are not stable when using a custom NPM registry with a port number

pnpm/pnpm#6778.body:
Hello,

I recently encountered an issue with pnpm version 8.6.6 during a Docker build process.

When running the command in a Dockerfile:
```
pnpm install --no-optional -r --offline --ignore-scripts --frozen-lockfile 
```
the command did not fail but led to an empty node_modules folder. 

I found that when running the same command interactively inside the Docker container, a confirmation prompt was shown. After confirming with Y, the command executed as expected.

However, this behavior is problematic in non-interactive environments like Docker builds, where the command cannot receive any user input.

As a workaround, I used the `--config.confirmModulesPurge=false` option to automatically confirm the prompt, as shown below:

```
pnpm install --no-optional -r --offline --ignore-scripts --frozen-lockfile --config.confirmModulesPurge=false
```

This successfully bypassed the confirmation and the node_modules folder was correctly populated.

Although I was able to find a workaround, I believe it would be helpful to improve the handling of this situation, perhaps by making the --no-optional flag non-interactive by default, or providing clear documentation about the need for --config.confirmModulesPurge=false in non-interactive environments.

Thank you for your attention to this issue!
pnpm/pnpm#6778.title: Non-Interactive pnpm install --no-optional hangs without --config.confirmModulesPurge=false

### pnpm version: `8.5.1`

### Code to reproduce the issue:

In a multi-package workspace where "does_not_exist" is a script in no packages.

```shell
$ pnpm -r run does_not_exist && echo "hi"
Scope: 19 of 20 workspace projects
None of the selected packages has a "does_not_exist" script
ha
```

### Expected behavior:

echo should never be executed

```shell
$ pnpm -r run does_not_exist && echo "hi"
Scope: 19 of 20 workspace projects
None of the selected packages has a "does_not_exist" script
```

### Actual behavior:

echo is executed, run does not fail.

From the [docs](https://pnpm.io/cli/run#--recursive--r):  **If none of the packages have the command, the command fails.**

```shell
$ pnpm -r run does_not_exist && echo "hi"
Scope: 19 of 20 workspace projects
None of the selected packages has a "does_not_exist" script
 ha
```

and an example in github actions: https://github.com/functionless/eventual/actions/runs/5616119079/job/15217864354?pr=395#step:5:2

 - `node -v` prints: v18.14.2
 - Windows, macOS, or Linux?: linux (WSL & github actions)

pnpm/pnpm#7087.body:
### Verify latest release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in the latest pnpm release

### pnpm version

7.33.6, 8.7.5

### Which area(s) of pnpm are affected? (leave empty if unsure)

_No response_

### Link to the code that reproduces this issue or a replay of the bug

_No response_

### Reproduction steps

```
pnpm i react@17.0.0
pnpm i react-dom@18.0.0
rm -rf node_modules
pnpm i
```

### Describe the Bug

After installing/removing/changing packages, pnpm will print peer dependency warnings:

```
$ pnpm i react-dom@18.0.0
Packages: +2
++
Progress: resolved 6, reused 4, downloaded 2, added 2, done

dependencies:
+ react-dom 18.0.0 (18.2.0 is available)

 WARN  Issues with peer dependencies found
.
└─┬ react-dom 18.0.0
  └── ✕ unmet peer react@^18.0.0: found 17.0.0

Done in 2.5s
```

However, after this pnpm never prints these warnings again until you actually install/remove/change a package or delete the lockfile:

```
$ pnpm i
Lockfile is up to date, resolution step is skipped
Already up to date
Done in 660ms
```

Even if you delete `node_modules` and pnpm reinstalls the packages it doesn't show it:

```
$ rm -rf node_modules; pnpm i
Lockfile is up to date, resolution step is skipped
Packages: +6
++++++
Progress: resolved 6, reused 6, downloaded 0, added 6, done

dependencies:
+ react 17.0.0
+ react-dom 18.0.0

Done in 710ms
```

Even with `strict-peer-dependencies=true`, if the lockfile is up to date then pnpm will happily install these packages without even printing any warnings.

`pnpm list` doesn't mention it either:

```
$ pnpm list
Legend: production dependency, optional only, dev only

foo@1.0.0 /home/simon/dc/foo

dependencies:
react 17.0.0
react-dom 18.0.0
```

Aside from the fact that there appears to be no way to check peer state once the lockfile is up to date, a massive issue with this is that if somebody else installs a package and pushes the lockfile with an invalid peer state (via git), pnpm will happily install this bad state for me and I will *never* have been warned about it, since it will only have printed the warning when the other dev installed it.

We've wasted hours trying to track down bugs that were already fixed but didn't realise that the peer hadn't been updated because pnpm wouldn't tell us.

### Expected Behavior

`pnpm i` and `pnpm list` should always print peer dependencies warnings, regardless of whether anything is being updated or not. Or at the very least, there should be some sort of `pnpm check` command that can be used to check this.

NOTE: `npm i` always prints peer dependency warnings. `pnpm` should do the same.

### Which Node.js version are you using?

16.20.1

### Which operating systems have you used?

- [ ] macOS
- [ ] Windows
- [X] Linux

### If your OS is a Linux based, which one it is? (Include the version if relevant)

Debian 11
pnpm/pnpm#7087.title: pnpm does not warn about issues with peer dependencies if the lockfile is up to date

pnpm/pnpm#8153.body:
### Contribution

- [ ] I'd be willing to implement this feature ([contributing guide](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md))

### Describe the user story

I help maintain Node.js, including Corepack. We’ve been searching for a way to provide interoperability with npm for Corepack users. npm has said that they won’t support Corepack or its `packageManager` field, but they’re open to the idea of a _similar_ field, that has some different characteristics that npm considers vital: semver ranges, flexibility on what to do when validation fails, validation for both the package manager and the runtime. This has led to a standardization effort with the OpenJS Foundation to define a new field called `devEngines`: https://github.com/openjs-foundation/package-metadata-interoperability-collab-space/issues/15. [npm is in the process of implementing support for it](https://github.com/openjs-foundation/package-metadata-interoperability-collab-space/issues/15#issuecomment-2140751719) (other than the `download` sub-field).

Once npm adds support, the plan is to propose support for this field to Corepack, as it would contain all the same information as `packageManager`. Corepack would probably continue to support `packageManager` for backward compatibility, but the hope is that if npm, Corepack and other package managers all standardize on support for `devEngines`, then users could be assured that they can validate the package managers used for their projects.

### Describe the solution you'd like

pnpm should also implement `devEngines` per https://github.com/openjs-foundation/package-metadata-interoperability-collab-space/issues/15, or as much of it as makes sense for pnpm (for example, I assume they might follow npm and ignore the download parts).

### Describe the drawbacks of your solution

I understand if pnpm wants to wait and see npm go first, to see this _actually_ ship; and then pnpm can copy or share code from npm.

I can’t promise that Corepack will definitely support `devEngines`, but the field’s whole purpose is to provide a configuration for both npm and Corepack to share so I expect it to happen.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Continue to support `packageManager` and its downsides, as shown by https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/issues/8087.
pnpm/pnpm#8153.title: Support `devEngines` field

### Contribution

- [ ] I'd be willing to implement this feature ([contributing guide](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md))

### Describe the user story

Rspack recently encountered a token theft attack where it seems that the npm classic token they used on CI is leaked and attackers are using this to publish a malicious version of the package. See https://github.com/web-infra-dev/rspack/issues/8767

Rspack uses npm provenance when publishing new versions using the CI. When the attacker is trying to publish the malicious version of the package, he/she/it doesn't have access to the CI, so the attacker publishes the package locally, resulting in this version not having attestation.

----

**Update**

On Aug 28, 2025, nx's publish token was compromised. Since the attackers have no access to the CI/CD infra, the malicious versions were published without provenance.

This once again shows how important this feature is.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Forbidding an already attested package from upgrading to an unattested version can reduce this kind of risk since the attackers need to have access to both the npm and the GitHub in order to publish an attested malicious version.

### Describe the drawbacks of your solution

It is possible that a maintainer deliberately disables npm provenance.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

pnpm/pnpm#9197.body:
### Contribution

- [ ] I'd be willing to implement this feature ([contributing guide](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md))

_Not sure, but I could try if I have time._

### Describe the user story

I had typed `-d` and was surprised to find out it didn't have any effect. Reading [the docs,](https://pnpm.io/cli/add#--save-dev--d) I found out that it must be uppercase `-D`.

### Describe the solution you'd like

I think the case shouldn't matter. There's no conflicting lowercase option anyway. Also, most Unix tools typically prefer to use lowercase shorthand options, reserving uppercase for more rarely used conflicting options.

Same can be for `-p`, `-o`, `-e` etc. And global (which is oddly `-g` despite being a more cautious option) could also accept `-G`.

### Describe the drawbacks of your solution

Future conflict of two different options sharing the same letter of the alphabet.

However, there are only five shorthand options right now, so we still have 21 unused alphabets. We should be fine.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

None.

### Verify latest release

### pnpm version

10.7.1

### Which area(s) of pnpm are affected? (leave empty if unsure)

Lockfile

### Link to the code that reproduces this issue or a replay of the bug

https://github.com/azu/pnpm--frozen-lockfile-bug

### Reproduction steps

`pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` does not throw an error when the lockfile is out of sync with pnpm-workspace.yaml's catalog.

## Reproduce Repository

- https://github.com/azu/pnpm--frozen-lockfile-bug

This repository's lockfile and catalog reference the package version as follows:

- `lodash@4.0.0` in the pnpm-workspace.yaml(catalog)
- `lodash@4.17.21` in the lockfile

### pnpm-workspace.yaml

- refer `lodash@4.0.0` in the workspace file

```yaml
catalog:
  lodash: 4.0.0
```

### pnpm-lock.yaml

- refer `lodash@4.17.21` in the lockfile

```yaml
lockfileVersion: '9.0'

settings:
  autoInstallPeers: true
  excludeLinksFromLockfile: false

catalogs:
  default:
    lodash:
      specifier: 4.17.21
      version: 4.17.21

importers:

  .:
    dependencies:
      lodash:
        specifier: 'catalog:'
        version: 4.17.21

packages:

  lodash@4.17.21:
    resolution: {integrity: sha512-v2kDEe57lecTulaDIuNTPy3Ry4gLGJ6Z1O3vE1krgXZNrsQ+LFTGHVxVjcXPs17LhbZVGedAJv8XZ1tvj5FvSg==}

snapshots:

  lodash@4.17.21: {}

```


## Reproduce Steps

1. Run `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` in the workspace root directory

```
git clone https://github.com/azu/pnpm--frozen-lockfile-bug
cd pnpm--frozen-lockfile-bug
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
```




### Describe the Bug

I expect that running `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` will throw an error when the lockfile is out of sync with the pnpm-workspace.yaml's catalog.
However, the command does not throw an error in `pnpm@10.7.1`.

## Actual Behavior

The `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` does not throw an error.
But, the command `pnpm install` updates the lockfile to match the pnpm-workspace.yaml.

```bash
pnpm install
git diff
diff --git i/pnpm-lock.yaml w/pnpm-lock.yaml
index 41fa5a5..7d83bb3 100644
--- i/pnpm-lock.yaml
+++ w/pnpm-lock.yaml
@@ -7,8 +7,8 @@ settings:
 catalogs:
   default:
     lodash:
-      specifier: 4.17.21
-      version: 4.17.21
+      specifier: 4.0.0
+      version: 4.0.0

 importers:

@@ -16,13 +16,13 @@ importers:
     dependencies:
       lodash:
         specifier: 'catalog:'
-        version: 4.17.21
+        version: 4.0.0

 packages:

-  lodash@4.17.21:
-    resolution: {integrity: sha512-v2kDEe57lecTulaDIuNTPy3Ry4gLGJ6Z1O3vE1krgXZNrsQ+LFTGHVxVjcXPs17LhbZVGedAJv8XZ1tvj5FvSg==}
+  lodash@4.0.0:
+    resolution: {integrity: sha512-bWpSlBobTcHYK9eUzcBYHhSBGzvSzEsxocnW5+v7p6wCRlY1icneTe2ACam3mGdAu82+RLL32cmyl7TRlJHqZw==}

 snapshots:

-  lodash@4.17.21: {}
+  lodash@4.0.0: {}
```

### Expected Behavior

The `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` should throw an error indicating that the lockfile is out of sync with the pnpm-workspace.yaml.


### Which Node.js version are you using?

v22.13.1

### Which operating systems have you used?

- [x] macOS
- [ ] Windows
- [x] Linux

### If your OS is a Linux based, which one it is? (Include the version if relevant)

No.

## Context

I met this issue when dependabot created a PR to update the lockfile and the catalog was not updated. (This looks like a bug in dependabot. https://github.com/dependabot/dependabot-core/issues/11953)

I expected that running `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` would throw an error, but it did not.
This behavior is unexpected and could lead to confusion when working with pnpm catalogs.

## Related Issue

Following issue is similar to this one, but I met this issue on pnpm catalogs.

- https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/issues/5794

pnpm/pnpm#9715.body:
### Verify latest release

- [x] I verified that the issue exists in the latest pnpm release

### pnpm version

10.12.4

### Which area(s) of pnpm are affected? (leave empty if unsure)

_No response_

### Link to the code that reproduces this issue or a replay of the bug

_No response_

### Reproduction steps

1. Make your user's home directory's `~/.local` be a symlink to another folder, instead of being a folder by itself. For example, make `/tmp/.local/` and symlink `~/.local` to it.
2. Install `pnpm@latest-10` using npm. Ensure that corepack is **not** installed
3. In another folder, create `package.json` that contains `{"packageManager":"pnpm@10.10.0"}`, some version that is **different** from the latest pnpm version
4. Run `pnpm --version`
5. Observe the error

### Describe the Bug

This error is returned when running `pnpm` with any command

```
 ERROR  Failed to switch pnpm to v10.10.0. Looks like pnpm CLI is missing at "/root/.local/share/pnpm/.tools/pnpm/10.10.0/bin" or is incorrect
spawnSync /root/.local/share/pnpm/.tools/pnpm/10.10.0/bin/pnpm ENOENT
```

Here is a transcript of reproducing it inside a fresh `node:22-slim` (Debian bookworm slim) container to ensure it's not affected by cruft from the host system:

```
cflee@cf-lee-tp92p ~ % docker run -it --rm --entrypoint /bin/bash node:22-slim
root@6fb614752c92:/# cd
root@6fb614752c92:~# ls -al
total 16
drwx------ 2 root root 4096 Jun 10 00:00 .
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root 4096 Jul  3 13:44 ..
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root  571 Apr 10  2021 .bashrc
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root  161 Jul  9  2019 .profile
root@6fb614752c92:~# mkdir /tmp/.local/
root@6fb614752c92:~# ln -s /tmp/.local .local
root@6fb614752c92:~# ls -al
total 16
drwx------ 1 root root 4096 Jul  3 13:44 .
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root 4096 Jul  3 13:44 ..
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root  571 Apr 10  2021 .bashrc
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root   11 Jul  3 13:44 .local -> /tmp/.local
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root  161 Jul  9  2019 .profile
root@6fb614752c92:~# mkdir proj
root@6fb614752c92:~# cd proj
root@6fb614752c92:~/proj# echo '{"packageManager":"pnpm@10.10.0"}' > package.json
root@6fb614752c92:~/proj# pnpm --version
bash: pnpm: command not found
root@6fb614752c92:~/proj# npm install -g pnpm@latest-10

added 1 package in 6s

1 package is looking for funding
  run `npm fund` for details
npm notice
npm notice New major version of npm available! 10.9.2 -> 11.4.2
npm notice Changelog: https://github.com/npm/cli/releases/tag/v11.4.2
npm notice To update run: npm install -g npm@11.4.2
npm notice
root@6fb614752c92:~/proj# pnpm --version
 ERROR  Failed to switch pnpm to v10.10.0. Looks like pnpm CLI is missing at "/root/.local/share/pnpm/.tools/pnpm/10.10.0/bin" or is incorrect
spawnSync /root/.local/share/pnpm/.tools/pnpm/10.10.0/bin/pnpm ENOENT
root@6fb614752c92:~/proj# ls -l /root/.local/share/pnpm/.tools/pnpm/10.10.0/bin/
total 8
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 103 Jul  3 13:45 pnpm -> ../../../../../../../../tmp/.local/share/pnpm/.tools/pnpm/10.10.0_tmp_24/node_modules/pnpm/bin/pnpm.cjs
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 103 Jul  3 13:45 pnpx -> ../../../../../../../../tmp/.local/share/pnpm/.tools/pnpm/10.10.0_tmp_24/node_modules/pnpm/bin/pnpx.cjs
root@6fb614752c92:~/proj# ls -l /root/.local/share/pnpm/.tools/pnpm/10.10.0/bin/../../../../../../../../tmp/.local/share/pnpm/.tools/pnpm/
total 4
drwxr-xr-x 4 root root 4096 Jul  3 13:45 10.10.0

```

In this case the symlink depth is correct and it can reach `/tmp/.local/`, so part of the issue is that inside the `{globalBinDir}/.tools/pnpm/` the symlink is referring to `10.10.0_tmp_24` instead of `10.10.0`.

In other cases, where the user's home directory and the directory that `~/.local/` is symlinked-to are at different depths, the symlink's `../../../../../../../../` is wrong and may not even reach `/` so it doesn't even reach the `{globalBinDir}/` successfully.

### Expected Behavior

`pnpm` can run without errors when the `~/.local` is a symlink.

Currently this is breaking the Renovate "full" self-hosted image's usage of `pnpm`, as in their containerbase base image the `~/.local` is a symlink. https://github.com/renovatebot/renovate/discussions/34726#discussioncomment-13570632

### Which Node.js version are you using?

v22.17.0

### Which operating systems have you used?

- [ ] macOS
- [ ] Windows
- [x] Linux

### If your OS is a Linux based, which one it is? (Include the version if relevant)

Debian bookworm

### Reproduction steps

### Describe the Bug

Notice how,

- When the package is added to the 1st project and saved to the catalog (step 2), it is added to the catalog with the semver range `^X.Y.Z` as expected, as PNPM adds by default: https://github.com/ravindUwU/repro-pnpm-pnpm-9759/commit/917b6e4b058c3f82a4a812000a0e57835816ecf9

- When the same package is added to the 2nd project the same way (step 3), the package version in `pnpm-workspace.yaml` changes from the previously-added semver range `^X.Y.Z`, to the fixed version `X.Y.Z`: https://github.com/ravindUwU/repro-pnpm-pnpm-9759/commit/3dd48b907c66690210f7c4d6d1844f9d6911221a

### Expected Behavior

When the package is added to the 2nd project as above,
- If a newer package version is available, the catalog is updated to the newer `^X.Y.Z` semver range.
- Otherwise (if the version already in the catalog is re-used), **the catalog remains unchanged** as `^X.Y.Z` (in this case) instead of being switched to the fixed version `X.Y.Z`.

### Reproduction steps

In [the linked repository](https://github.com/TrevorBurnham/pnpm-frozen-lockfile-bug-repro), run this command:

```
CI=true pnpm install
```

### Describe the Bug

When the env var `CI` is `true`, `pnpm install` throws an error if the `pnpmfileChecksum` in `pnpm-lock.yaml` doesn't match the contents of the pnpmfile:

> ERR_PNPM_LOCKFILE_CONFIG_MISMATCH  Cannot proceed with the frozen installation. The current "pnpmfileChecksum" configuration doesn't match the value found in the lockfile

That's perfectly consistent with the [documented behavior of `--frozen-lockfile`](https://pnpm.io/cli/install#--frozen-lockfile). However, what's strange is how hard to override this behavior is:

* ❌ `CI=true npm_config_frozen_lockfile=false pnpm install` fails
* ❌ `CI=true pnpm_config_frozen_lockfile=false pnpm install` fails
* ❌ Using the `updateConfig` hook in `pnpmfile.js` to set `frozenLockfile: false` fails.
* ✅ Creating a `.npmrc` with `frozen-lockfile = false` works.
* ✅ `CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile=false` works.
* ✅ `CI=true pnpm install --no-frozen-lockfile` works.

### Expected Behavior

I'd expect setting `frozenLockfile` via an env var or the `updateConfig` hook to have the same effect as setting it via `.npmrc` or a CLI flag.

v22.17.1

pnpm/pnpm#9884.body:
### Verify latest release

- [x] I verified that the issue exists in the latest pnpm release

### pnpm version

10.15.0

### Which area(s) of pnpm are affected? (leave empty if unsure)

_No response_

### Link to the code that reproduces this issue or a replay of the bug

https://github.com/mxxk-examples/pnpm-v10-config-issue

### Reproduction steps

1. Check out https://github.com/mxxk-examples/pnpm-v10-config-issue.

2. Build the Docker container:

    ```bash
    docker build -t pnpm-repro .
    ```

3. Inspect the results in each reproduction directory within the Docker container:

    ```bash
    docker run --rm -it pnpm-repro sh
    ```

### Describe the Bug

Suppose the intention is to append the following line to the current project's `.npmrc` file:

```
//npm.pkg.github.com/:_authToken=${NPM_TOKEN}
```

Doing so is useful in CI/CD where an `NPM_TOKEN` environment variable is provided for accessing a private NPM repository (in this example, the [GitHub NPM registry](https://docs.github.com/en/packages/working-with-a-github-packages-registry/working-with-the-npm-registry)).

In pnpm v9, the command

```
pnpm config set \
    --location=project \
    '//npm.pkg.github.com/:_authToken=${NPM_TOKEN}'
```

did just that.

However, in pnpm v10, the same command does two different things, depending on whether there is an `.npmrc` file already present in the project directory:

- If there is no `.npmrc` file present, it writes the following to `pnpm-workspace.yaml`:

    ```yaml
    //npmPkgGithubCom/:AuthToken: ${NPM_TOKEN}
    ```

- If there is an `.npmrc` file present, it writes the following to `.npmrc`:

    ```
    npm-pkg-github-com-auth-token=${NPM_TOKEN}
    ```

Lastly, if the `pnpm config set` command is run with `--location=global`, all three examples (pnpm v9, pnpm v10 without `.npmrc`, pnpm v10 with `.npmrc`) produce the same result: they write the following to `~/.npmrc`:

```
//npm.pkg.github.com/:_authToken=${NPM_TOKEN}
```

### Expected Behavior

I'm not sure what should be considered correct behavior in this case. The option being set is not pnpm-specific, so using the `npm config set` command instead may be the right move.

To my knowledge, however, the behavior demonstrated above is not documented anywhere, nor is it called out as a breaking change in pnpm v10.

### Which Node.js version are you using?

v24.6.0

### Which operating systems have you used?

- [x] macOS
- [ ] Windows
- [x] Linux

### If your OS is a Linux based, which one it is? (Include the version if relevant)

Alpine (Docker)

### Contribution

### Describe the user story

A user wants to set an array value such as [`minimumReleaseAgeExclude`](https://pnpm.io/settings#minimumreleaseageexclude) globally with a simple command, without having to remember [the global config file locations](https://pnpm.io/cli/config#:~:text=The%20global%20configuration%20file) (`~/AppData/Local/pnpm/config/rc`, `~/Library/Preferences/pnpm/rc` or `~/.config/pnpm/rc`) for the platform they are on

### Describe the solution you'd like

`pnpm config path --global` could print the location for the global config file, so that the file can be edited dynamically with something like `echo`:

```bash
echo -e "minimum-release-age-exclude[]=webpack\nminimum-release-age-exclude[]=react" >> "$(pnpm config path --global)"
```

<details>

<summary>Show YAML version</summary>

Probably YAML is not supported as of pnpm v10 in the global config file, but this is the version that I first started with

```bash
echo -e "minimumReleaseAgeExclude:\n  - webpack\n  - react" >> "$(pnpm config path --global)"
```

</details>

`pnpm config path` without the `--global` flag (if it should be supported) would probably follow the same config resolution and print:

1. the local config file path if it exists
2. the global config file path if the local config file doesn't exist

### Describe the drawbacks of your solution

It is not a single-command solution to setting array values

### Describe alternatives you've considered

[`pnpm config set` could allow setting array values](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/issues/10015):

```bash
$ pnpm config set "minimumReleaseAgeExclude[]" webpack
$ pnpm config set "minimumReleaseAgeExclude[]" react
```

### Contribution

- [ ] I'd be willing to implement this feature ([contributing guide](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md))

### Describe the user story

When implementing `minimumReleaseAge`, the current state of the repository might not conform to that setting.

For example:

* A package `package` was updated in the repository
* That package version was uploaded 1 day ago
* The repository sets `minimumReleaseAge` to 7 days

In this situation, we have these workarounds:

- Downgrade `package`
- Wait for 6 days
- Add a `minimumReleaseAgeExclude` for `package`
- Reduce the `minimumReleaseAge`

### Describe the solution you'd like

Instead, it would be better if we could allow the current state of the repository (but not future states) to be spared using `minimumReleaseAgeExclude`.

```yaml
minimumReleaseAgeExclude:
  - package@9.3.4
```

This way, no need to downgrade a package, no need to wait for a week, no need to exclude future versions (which we want `minimumReleaseAgeExclude` to be enforced on) and no need to reduce `minimumReleaseAge`.

### Describe the drawbacks of your solution

_No response_

### Describe alternatives you've considered

- Downgrade `package`
- Wait for 6 days
- Add a `minimumReleaseAgeExclude` for `package`
- Reduce the `minimumReleaseAge`

pnpm/pnpm#10020.body:
### Contribution

- [ ] I'd be willing to implement this feature ([contributing guide](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md))

### Describe the user story

As a developer, I'd like to be able to easily check if specific packages are part of the dependency tree described by `pnpm-lock.yaml`, and if they would be installed when running `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`, even if those packages are not currently installed (for example before running `pnpm install` or after changing branches).

Currently `pnpm list` seems to only list currently installed packages under `node_modules`. If you remove `node_modules/` then `pnpm list <packages...>` will not find anything even if those packages are referenced in `package.json` or `pnpm-lock.yaml`.

This is especially relevant now due to the recent npm supply-chain attacks. Having this new option would make it easier and faster to check if your lockfile contains malicious packages.

### Describe the solution you'd like

npm has the option [--package-lock-only](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v11/commands/npm-ls#package-lock-only) for `npm list`. I want a similar option for pnpm, but the naming should probably be `--lockfile-only` as that is already available for `pnpm install` (https://pnpm.io/cli/install#--lockfile-only)

### Describe the drawbacks of your solution

_No response_

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Currently when I want to check for example if my `pnpm-lock.yaml` in some branch/commit includes some known malicious packages I need to do something like this:

```bash
# switch to commit where I want to check the lockfile
git switch --detach <ref>
# remove all node_modules directories under my repo to get a clean state before pnpm install
pnpm dlx rimraf -g '**/node_modules/' -I
# run pnpm install so I can use pnpm list
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --ignore-scripts
pnpm list -r --depth Infinity <packages...>
```

But this can be very slow depending on the number of dependencies in your project.

If pnpm list had this feature similar to `npm list --package-lock-only` then I could just do this instead:

```shell
# switch to commit where I want to check the lockfile
git switch --detach <ref>
pnpm list -r --depth Infinity --lockfile-only <packages...>
```

There might be other ways to parse `pnpm-lock.yaml` directly but the pnpm CLI should have an easy way to do this without external scripts/tools.
pnpm/pnpm#10020.title: Add `--lockfile-only` option to `pnpm list`

### Contribution

- [x] I'd be willing to implement this feature ([contributing guide](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md))

### Describe the user story

In Bun's blog [Behind The Scenes of Bun Install](https://bun.com/blog/behind-the-scenes-of-bun-install), they described how they make `bun install` fast.

We will ignore all their "reducing syscalls" or "async dns resolution only for macOS" bullshit from that post. Since we already know that one of the biggest performance overheads is the Network, they mentioned using 64 concurrent network connections by default:

> Bun maintains a pool of 64(!) concurrent HTTP connections (configurable via BUN_CONFIG_MAX_HTTP_REQUESTS) on dedicated network threads.

pnpm currently sets `network-concurrency` to 16 by default.

### Describe the solution you'd like

pnpm could implement two new configurations replacing the existing `network-concurrency` option: `min-network-concurrency` and `max-network-concurrency`. pnpm will start with `max-network-concurrency`, and when it hits `TimeoutError`, or `Read Stream EOF`, or any similar network error, pnpm will scale back network concurrency until reaching `min-network-concurrency`.

### Describe the drawbacks of your solution

pnpm has way wider adoption than Bun. While Bun can increase their network concurrency to 64, DoS-ing registry w/o significant consequences, this doesn't apply to pnpm. pnpm is way more popular, and the impact will be noticeable. 

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

pnpm/pnpm#10076.body:
### Contribution

- [ ] I'd be willing to implement this feature ([contributing guide](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md))

### Describe the user story

As has come to the ecosystem's attention numerous times in the past year, NPM packages are a worrying vector for supply chain attacks. While it is possible to introduce malicious code into the runtime of a package (such as cookie stealers and crypto miners for frontend applications, or full-on malware for Node applications), it is much more reliable for an adversary to simply add an install hook script (such as `postinstall`) to the package and deliver the payload immediately.

pnpm has already implemented some useful security measures to prevent the largest attack vectors. Specifically: 

#### `onlyBuiltDependencies`
`onlyBuiltDependencies` requires that any packages with install hooks be approved explicitly by the maintainer.

This, however, still leaves a security hole when packages in `onlyBuiltDependencies` are hijacked. [When the package `nx` was compromised](https://www.wiz.io/blog/s1ngularitys-aftermath) using what appeared to be a series of exploits in their GitHub Actions workflows around August 26th of this year (2025), it reminded us that even packages trusted with install hook permissions can still be compromised. Hence, the next mitigation:

#### `minimumReleaseAge`
`minimumReleaseAge` helps prevent the attack above by allowing maintainers to specify a minimum age for a package release before pnpm will install it. The hope here is that the ecosystem will identify the compromised package quickly and NPM and/or the maintainers will remove it from the registry promptly (which has happened fairly consistently with every attack I've heard about). However, this solution requires manual setup and is effectively just a race condition—which worries me on both fronts.

### Describe the solution you'd like

**Tie `onlyBuiltDependencies` to the package's version/hash**

Since compromised NPM packages inherently must differ in file content, requiring each new version to be approved manually would introduce additional barriers to initial compromise. How to track this internally and how to present this to the user are to be workshopped. My instinct is to convert the existing `onlyBuiltDependencies` from an array to a record, keyed by package name with values that are either a pinned version (no version ranges), the package's hash, or both.

I would expect this barrier to have slightly worse returns than the existing `onlyBuiltDependencies` implementation, since package updates are likely much more frequent an operation than package addition, and, if the barrier `onlyBuiltDependencies` is effective at all, users must take manual action to audit their dependencies. Once initially audited, I'd imagine a larger portion of users would become complacent in trusting that the updated package was not hijacked.

It may also be a good idea to display the relative publish time for a package versions pending build approval.

I am not at all tied to a specific implementation for this suggestion—anything that increases the security of pnpm users while not introducing *too* much developer friction is a win in my book!

### Describe the drawbacks of your solution

- Additional friction in developer workflow for dependencies with install hooks
- Likely needs a revamped `pnpm approve-builds` interface
- Depending on the final implementation, and if this becomes the default behavior, this could be a breaking change
  - May include adapting old configs to a new format

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_
pnpm/pnpm#10076.title: Tie `onlyBuiltDependencies` to package version/hash

### Contribution

- [ ] I'd be willing to implement this feature ([contributing guide](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md))

### Describe the user story

Continuing from [Issue 9102](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/issues/9102)

Having a flag like `--allow-all-builds` makes sense. Consider the following command to create a nuxt project -
```
npm create nuxt@latest
```
It first creates the project folder, and then asks which package manager do I want to use to install the dependencies. `@parcel/watcher`, and `esbuild` scripts are not allowed, and hence ignored. Since, I don't even have the `package.json` yet, I can't put anything to it which will allow the builds.

I can't run the command `npm create nuxt@latest` again, because then it will be a new nuxt project. I can't even use `pnpm approve-builds`, because now it doesn't show any ignored approve-builds. Only option left for me is to use `npm` package manager, which defeats the purpose having `pnpm`.

```
D:\Projects>npm create nuxt@latest

> npx
> create-nuxt

√ Which package manager would you like to use?
pnpm

o Installing dependencies...

╭ Warning ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                            │
│   Ignored build scripts: @parcel/watcher, esbuild.                                         │
│   Run "pnpm approve-builds" to pick which dependencies should be allowed to run scripts.   │
│                                                                                            │
╰────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯

Done in 17.2s using pnpm v10.18.3
```

### Describe the solution you'd like

Have `--allow-all-builds` for allowing all the builds while running the commands which can't be run otherwise again on the same thing

### Contribution

- [ ] I'd be willing to implement this feature ([contributing guide](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md))

### Describe the user story

Users who will not create packages (but create just applications) do not need fields in package.json:

- name
- version
- description
- main
- keywords
- author
- license

Especially, the license field is harmful for organizations or beginners; they may unintentionally mistakenly license their repositories under the ISC license.
These fields are not necessary unless you are going to publish the repository and waste of time to be maintained.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Add an option like `--minimal`:

```
$ pnpm init --init-type module --minimal
Wrote to path/to/package.json

{
  "scripts": {
    "test": "echo \"Error: no test specified\" && exit 1"
  },
  "packageManager": "pnpm@10.22.0",
  "type": "module"
}
```

```
$ pnpm init --help
Version 10.22.0 (compiled to binary; bundled Node.js v24.11.1)
Usage: pnpm init

Create a package.json file

Options:
      --init-package-manager         Pin the project to the current pnpm version by adding a "packageManager" field to package.json
      --init-type <commonjs|module>  Set the module system for the package. Defaults to "commonjs".
      --minimal                      Strip unnecessary fields for applications in package.json

Visit https://pnpm.io/10.x/cli/init for documentation about this command.
```

### Describe the drawbacks of your solution

None

### Describe alternatives you've considered

1. `pnpm init`
2. `vim package.json` (or your favorite editor)
3. Strip extra fields (`dd` (cut the current line) multiple times)
4. `:wq` (save and exit Vim)

### Describe the Bug

Corepack tries to run `pnpm.cjs`, but the compiled file is now `pnpm.mjs`:


pnpm/pnpm#10244.body:
Another note on this subject -- I ran into problems when using `engines.runtime` or `devEngines.runtime` in the root `package.json` in a monorepo, without any Node installed in the system.  The issue is that `pnpm install` fails due to post-install scripts not finding `node`.  In that state, there was no `node_modules/.bin` in the root yet.

This seems to be alleviated by removing `engines.runtime` from the root `package.json` and using `useNodeVersion: 24.11.1` in `pnpm-workspace.yaml`.  So, only using `engines.runtime` and `devEngines.runtime` in individual workspace `package.json`.

I just wanted to mention this in case this does not align with the expected usage.

_Originally posted by @markedwards in https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/discussions/10172#discussioncomment-15096590_

### Contribution

- [x] I'd be willing to implement this feature ([contributing guide](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md))

### Describe the user story

In a pnpm workspace I would like to pin certain dependencies e.g. rarely used CLIs etc. to a specific version.
I don't want to add them as normal `devDependencies` since they are not used often enough to justify always installing them.

The only solution currently is to inline the version in the command e.g. `pnpm dlx large-cli@1.2.3` or write a wrapper script that reads the version from the catalog definition.

Especially in the current situation with lots of malicious npm packages, I think it is important to have an easy and scalable way of pinning the versions of packages, invoked via `dlx`.

### Describe the solution you'd like

When running `pnpm dlx` inside a pnpm workspace, resolve package versions from the catalog when using the `catalog:` protocol.

pnpm-workspace.yaml
```yaml
catalog:
  large-cli: '1.2.3'
```

`pnpm dlx large-cli@catalog:` -> should run `large-cli` with version `1.2.3`, no the latest.

### Describe the drawbacks of your solution

_No response_

### Describe alternatives you've considered

- Inlining the pinned version: would loose support for tooling like renovate, dependabot etc.
- wrapper script, that manually reads the catalog: not very ergonomic / scalable

pnpm/pnpm#10259.body:
This PR fixes a behaviour that I described in #9963. 
When setting the `trustPolicy` to `no-downgrade` all packages are checked for a time field.
The check occurs before the `trustPolicyExclude` setting takes effect so there is no way to exclude
packages from repositories that don't support the time field from being checked.

To fix this I moved the exclude check so that it runs before we're checking for the time field in the package metadata.
pnpm/pnpm#10259.title: fix: don't check package's time field when it's excluded in trust policy

10.22

Dependencies resolver

### Link to the code that reproduces this issue or a replay of the bug

_No response_

### Reproduction steps

BEFORE
- using next through a catalog + having:
```
minimumReleaseAge: 20160
next_v15_current:
    '@next/bundle-analyzer': 15.5.2
    eslint-config-next: 15.5.2
    next: 15.5.2
```
- pnpm.lock had a reference to optionalDependencies of next: @next/swc-(arch)

AFTER
bumping next following the https://react.dev/blog/2025/12/03/critical-security-vulnerability-in-react-server-components#update-next-js
```
next_v15_current:
    '@next/bundle-analyzer': 15.5.7
    eslint-config-next: 15.5.7
    next: 15.5.7

minimumReleaseAgeExclude:
  - 'next@15.5.7'
```

`pnpm -i` complained about a SINGLE `@next/something` related package being too recent -> added it to minimumReleaseAgeExclude

THEN pnpm stopped complaining BUT dropped the @next/swc-(arch) packages from pnpm.lock

subsequent reinstalls with --frozen-lockfile crashed due to the missing packages, but a normal `pnpm i` even `pnpm i --force` refuses to add the missing packages (likely due to not passing minimumReleaseAgeExclude)

SOLUTION
once I added a blanket exclusion:
```
minimumReleaseAgeExclude:
  - '@next/*'
  - 'next@15.5.7'
```

pnpm re-added the missing packages to pnpm.lock ✅ 

This is NOT correct, pnpm should not silently drop optional packages, it should error and prompt them to be added to `minimumReleaseAgeExclude`. "Optional" in this case means "depending on the arch", they're REQUIRED for the build to work.

### Describe the Bug

pnpm should not silently drop optional packages not passing `minimumReleaseAgeExclude`, it should error and prompt them to be added to `minimumReleaseAgeExclude`

NOTE: the dropped deps were optionalDependencies, the `@next/swc-*` transitively required by `next -> @next/swc`

This caused next build to fail since swc is used to compile

### Expected Behavior

pnpm should error and prompt them to be added to `minimumReleaseAgeExclude`. It did it for 2 packages but silently dropped 6 other (optionalDependencies)

v22.18.0

- [ ] Windows

### Contribution

- [x] I'd be willing to implement this feature ([contributing guide](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md))

### Describe the user story

I created the pnpm workspace manifest file with a dot in front, as I am used to tools like Prettier and ESLint that prefix their configuration files with a dot. It took me a few minutes to figure out the file shouldn't have a dot.

### Describe the solution you'd like

It appears pnpm already detects and has an error message for manifest files with the wrong name.

https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/blob/76718b32ad333bdda2602cd8728da0b5bfa195c0/workspace/find-workspace-dir/src/index.ts#L8-L19

I propose adding the two aforementioned file names to the list of file names to warn other users that might make the same mistake I did.

### Describe the drawbacks of your solution

If pnpm makes either file name valid in the future, then the error message would serve to confuse users that used a prior version of pnpm where those file names are invalid.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Allow `.pnpm-workspace.yml` and `.pnpm-workspace.yaml` to be valid names for the manifest file. However, I do not believe this would be consistent with existing behavior. `pnpm-workspace.yml` (instead of `.yaml`) is already an invalid file name with an associated error message as highlighted earlier.

### Describe the Bug

The `pnpm add` [`--allow-build=<pkg>` flag](https://pnpm.io/cli/add#--allow-build) (implemented in [PR 9086](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/pull/9086)) writes lines in `pnpm-workspace.yaml` to [the non-preferred `onlyBuiltDependencies` setting](https://pnpm.io/settings#onlybuiltdependencies) instead of [the preferred `allowBuilds` setting](https://pnpm.io/blog/releases/10.26#allowbuilds:~:text=This%20replaces%20onlyBuiltDependencies%20and%20ignoredBuiltDependencies%20as%20the%20preferred%20configuration%20method)

### Expected Behavior

`allowBuilds` is used exclusively, because [the pnpm 10.26 blog post](https://pnpm.io/blog/releases/10.26#allowbuilds:~:text=This%20replaces%20onlyBuiltDependencies%20and%20ignoredBuiltDependencies%20as%20the%20preferred%20configuration%20method) mentions that it is the preferred configuration method

> This replaces `onlyBuiltDependencies` and `ignoredBuiltDependencies` as the preferred configuration method

pnpm/pnpm#10409.body: Using the v8 serialization format rather than JSON for caches (https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/pull/9971) seems to have caused a 2.33x performance regression to the `resolveDependenciesTree` function on v11. I noticed this when testing pnpm against a large repro I use at work and observing `pnpm install` consistently take much longer than it does on v10. ## Measurements I profiled the `resolveDependenciesTree` function before and after commit `9b344c898`. Commit `d392c3d05` is the parent commit of `9b344c898`. | Test | Trial 1 | Trial 2 | Trial 3 | Trial 4 | Trial 5 | Average (ms) | | ---------------------- | --------- | --------- | --------- | --------- | -------- | ------------- | | JSON (d392c3d05) | 19,758.14 | 20,165.70 | 20,887.73 | 20,538.55 | 21304.28 | **20,530.88** | | V8 buffers (9b344c898) | 46,971.59 | 47,418.23 | 47004.95 | 50838.92 | 46840.28 | **47,814.79** | The changes I made to support profiling can be found here: https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/commit/a50b919e453e925fe53d7bd4c277fec4f1394f1a Each test run had a warmup installation and was done from a fully populated pnpm store. ### Specs MacBook Pro (16-inch, Nov 2023) Chip: M3 Pro Memory 36 GB Node.js v24.11.0 macOS 26.2 ## CPU Profiles Looking at a left-heavy CPU profile, the `readV8FileStrictAsync` and `v8.deserialize` calls take around ~33s. This compares to ~7s for `JSON.parse` in the prior commit. This was comparing 2 CPU profiles of the ~10 that I took.

```sh
Done in 424ms using pnpm v10.27.0
$ cat package.json
{

  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "",

  "scripts": {
    "test": "echo \"Error: no test specified\" && exit 1"
  },
  "keywords": [],
  "author": "",
  "license": "ISC",
  "packageManager": "pnpm@10.27.0",
  "devDependencies": {
    "@std/html": "jsr:^1.0.5"
  },
  "peerDependencies": {
    "@std/html": "jsr:^1.0.5"
  }
}
$ pnpm add sass
 ERR_PNPM_INVALID_PEER_DEPENDENCY_SPECIFICATION  The peerDependencies field named '@std/html' of package 'pnpm-reproduction-peer-dep-sources' has an invalid value: 'jsr:^1.0.5'

The values in peerDependencies should be either a valid semver range, a `workspace:` spec, or a `catalog:` spec
```

### Describe the Bug

When running `pnpm add <source>:<package> --save-peer` the package is added to the `package.json` like this:

```patch
  "devDependencies": {
+   "<package>": "<source>:<version>"
  },
  "peerDependencies": {
+   "<package>": "<source>:<version>"
  }
```

As per [this comment](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/issues/9017#issuecomment-2639560013), the current behavior is incorrect, since source specifiers are not allowed in the version constraints of `peerDependencies`:

> I don't know why it worked in the past. peer dependencies were always meant to only be semver specs. Someone noticed the issue and it was fixed

Indeed, running `pnpm add <package>` will then result in an error:

```
 ERR_PNPM_INVALID_PEER_DEPENDENCY_SPECIFICATION  The peerDependencies field named '@std/html' of package 'pnpm-reproduction-peer-dep-sources' has an invalid value: 'jsr:^1.0.5'

The values in peerDependencies should be either a valid semver range, a `workspace:` spec, or a `catalog:` spec
```

### Expected Behavior

When running `pnpm add <source>:<package> --save-peer`, only the version constraint should be added to  the `peerDependencies` array of the `package.json`, while the full `<source>:<version>` should be added to the `devDependencies` as normal:

```patch
  "devDependencies": {
    "<package>": "<source>:<version>"
  },
  "peerDependencies": {
-   "<package>": "<source>:<version>"
+   "<package>": "<version>"
  }
```

pnpm/pnpm#10535.body:
### Verify latest release

- [x] I verified that the issue exists in the latest pnpm release

### pnpm version

10.28.0

### Which area(s) of pnpm are affected? (leave empty if unsure)

_No response_

### Link to the code that reproduces this issue or a replay of the bug

_No response_

### Reproduction steps

We have been investigating this over at https://github.com/NixOS/nixpkgs/issues/484013

That issue contains reproduction steps, but they are also present here in more detail:

1. [Install Nix/NixOS](https://nixos.org/download/)
2. Clone https://github.com/diogotcorreia/dotfiles
3. Checkout `b2f244d1b121353c4daee973818f551ade594e68`
4. In a separate terminal, run `./reproduce-pnpm-race.sh` (see below). This will simulate a slow I/O device (see aforementioned issue for reasoning, but tldr, this is happening in GitHub Actions)
5. In the git checkout, run `nix build --store <path from script output from step 4> .#book-metadata-api.pnpmDeps -L`
6. Observe that build fails due to mismatching hash (store is different than what is expected)
   ```
   error: hash mismatch in fixed-output derivation '/nix/store/q745430xvhjpy9y4bbs2p7p8w0nhy61j-book-metadata-api-pnpm-deps.drv':
            specified: sha256-isco9zsgbzfG+nHvEXNiGoRaDxeRhIYzmrNsrw9oyec=
               got:    sha256-gyJSIVay8us/LSYTSYKliz7oCzBwSdPe2UQa62XMfh4=
   ```
7. (cleanup) Run the cleanup commands shown in the output from step 4
To validate that this is caused by the slow I/O device, run step 5 again but without running step 4. You might have to pass `--rebuild` if you have successfully build it once already.

Note: if the build is succeeding even with the slow I/O device, try changing the `DELAY` value in the script to a higher value (e.g., `10`). I have managed to reproduce with a value of just `1`, but it might depend on network conditions as well.

`reproduce-pnpm-race.sh`:
```sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash

SUDO="sudo"
# If 1 does not work to reproduce the problem, try setting it to 10 or a number in between.
DELAY="1"

img="$(mktemp)"
echo "Creating image on $img"

# Create 1GB img file
dd if=/dev/zero of="$img" bs=1M count=1000
device="$($SUDO losetup --show --find "$img")"
echo "Mounted to $device"

$SUDO mkfs.ext4 "$device"

blocksize="$($SUDO blockdev --getsize "$device")"
echo "Blocksize: $blocksize"

echo "Creating /dev/mapper/dm-slow..."
echo "0 $blocksize delay $device 0 $DELAY" | $SUDO dmsetup create dm-slow

storeDir="$(mktemp -d)"
mntDir="$storeDir/nix/var/nix/builds"

mkdir -p "$mntDir"
$SUDO mount -o dirsync /dev/mapper/dm-slow "$mntDir"
$SUDO chown "$USER" "$mntDir"
echo "Mounted /dev/mapper/dm-slow at $mntDir"

echo
echo
echo "You can now run:"
echo "nix build --store $storeDir <installables>"

echo
echo
echo "To undo device setup, run the following command:"
echo "$SUDO umount $mntDir ; $SUDO dmsetup remove dm-slow ; $SUDO losetup -d $device ; rmdir $storeDir ; rm $img"
```

### Describe the Bug

Again, see https://github.com/NixOS/nixpkgs/issues/484013 for more detail, but tldr:

The `initStore` function in `worker/src/start.ts` exits early if any of the `mkdir` calls fail, which means that it is possible for the resulting store to be missing a few (empty) directories. While in normal circumstances this wouldn't be a problem, when packaging with Nix we need the resulting store to be bit-by-bit reproducible every time, otherwise it results in hash mismatches as we are seeing here.

Related code:
https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/blob/91a241e692de524a974460f69c35a309769d3045/worker/src/start.ts#L197-L215

The comment inside the catch block describes the exact problem: it is possible for a parallel process to also be creating a subset of these directories, and therefore it might cause an error to be thrown when calling `mkdirSync` (pnpm was patched to print the error):
```
Error: EEXIST: file already exists, mkdir '/build/tmp.fnIj9xZOAv/v10/files/df'
    at Object.mkdirSync (node:fs:1363:26)
    at initStore (/nix/store/isxzyd9rcsz6ik4074apl5x6dpjbsfxn-pnpm-10.28.0/libexec/pnpm/dist/worker.js:9888:28)
    at MessagePort.handleMessage (/nix/store/isxzyd9rcsz6ik4074apl5x6dpjbsfxn-pnpm-10.28.0/libexec/pnpm/dist/worker.js:9772:53)
    at [nodejs.internal.kHybridDispatch] (node:internal/event_target:845:20)
    at MessagePort.<anonymous> (node:internal/per_context/messageport:23:28) {
  errno: -17,
  code: 'EEXIST',
  syscall: 'mkdir',
  path: '/build/tmp.fnIj9xZOAv/v10/files/df'
}
```

However, this means that some (empty) directories [might be missing from the resulting store](https://github.com/NixOS/nixpkgs/issues/484013#issuecomment-3814859466).

### Expected Behavior

I would expect the resulting store to be bit-by-bit reproducible every time, regardless of I/O delays.
In particular, the `initStore` function should ignore when `mkdirSync` fails and keep creating the remaining directories.

### Which Node.js version are you using?

22.21.1

### Which operating systems have you used?

- [ ] macOS
- [ ] Windows
- [x] Linux

### If your OS is a Linux based, which one it is? (Include the version if relevant)

NixOS 25.11

### Verify latest release

- [x] I verified that the issue exists in the latest pnpm release

### pnpm version

_No response_

### Which area(s) of pnpm are affected? (leave empty if unsure)

_No response_

### Link to the code that reproduces this issue or a replay of the bug

_No response_

### Reproduction steps

1. Globally install the latest version of pnpm
2. In a folder, set `packageManager` to an older version of pnpm. Also create a package.json with a large amount of dependencies.
3. Run `pnpm i` (this will cause `pnpm` to install and start the older version of pnpm via https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/blob/47e850180adcde91978f12a6513218ade26857f4/pnpm/src/main.ts#L119.
4. `htop` or similar to find the "older" pnpm (spawned by the "newer" pnpm). Kill this pnpm with `SIGKILL` (e.g. `kill -9 <pid>`)
5. Observe that the exit code of the "newer" (outer) pnpm is 0, not 137 (which should be the exit code for a sigkilled process).

This is obviously a bit of a weird reproduction (why would one do this!), but it happens naturally due to the OOM killer. If the inner pnpm runs out of memory, the Linux OOMKiller will kill it with `SIGKILL`. That will trigger this scenario where the outer pnpm exits with code 0.

### Describe the Bug

I've already figured out the root cause, so I'll just describe that:

pnpm uses `spawn.sync` to invoke the correct pnpm version in this file:
https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/blob/47e850180adcde91978f12a6513218ade26857f4/pnpm/src/switchCliVersion.ts#L41

`cross-spawn` unfortunately does not return a `status` (it returns `status: undefined`) when the process is killed by a signal. Instead `signal` is set to a string (like `"SIGKILL"`). Because pnpm returns the `status` only, even if it is unset, and line 54 defaults to `0` if `status` is unset, the outer pnpm will always exit with code 0 if the inner pnpm is killed by `SIGKILL`.

To fix this, pnpm should conditionally call `process.exit(result.status ?? 0)` or `process.kill(process.pid, result.signal)` depending on whether `result.signal` is set.

### Expected Behavior

In the described case, pnpm should exit with status 137.

### Which Node.js version are you using?

does not matter

### Which operating systems have you used?

- [ ] macOS
- [ ] Windows
- [x] Linux

### If your OS is a Linux based, which one it is? (Include the version if relevant)

Ubuntu

Installing dependencies for some projects works with frozen lockfile on 10.28.2 but not on 10.29.1:
```console
cdxgen ❯ npx pnpm@10.29.1 install --frozen-lockfile
```

pnpm/pnpm#10592.body:
Closes #9088

Adds `pnpm sbom` for generating Software Bill of Materials in CycloneDX 1.7 JSON or SPDX 2.3 JSON.

```
pnpm sbom --sbom-format cyclonedx
pnpm sbom --sbom-format spdx
pnpm sbom --sbom-format cyclonedx --lockfile-only
```

Follows the `pnpm licenses` architecture — a core library (`@pnpm/sbom`) and a thin CLI plugin (`@pnpm/plugin-commands-sbom`). Walks the lockfile via `lockfileWalkerGroupImporterSteps` and reads package.json from the CAFS store for license/author metadata. `--lockfile-only` skips the store entirely. Supports `--prod`/`--dev`/`--no-optional` filtering and workspace `--filter`.

One new dependency: `spdx-license-ids` for license classification. Output validated against official CycloneDX 1.7 and SPDX 2.3 JSON schemas.

From there:

1. Add `packages/c/package.json` with a dependency on `typescript` for `^5.8.0-beta`

Adding a new `^5.8.0-beta` specifier to a third package (e.g. c) should resolve to the existing `5.8.0-beta` version.

pnpm/pnpm#10698.body:
### Verify latest release

- [x] I verified that the issue exists in the latest pnpm release

### pnpm version

10.30.2

### Which area(s) of pnpm are affected? (leave empty if unsure)

Store, Hooks

### Link to the code that reproduces this issue or a replay of the bug

_No response_

### Reproduction steps

1. Set up a project with `@pnpm/plugin-types-fixer` as a `configDependency` in `pnpm-workspace.yaml`:

```yaml
configDependencies:
  '@pnpm/plugin-types-fixer': '0.1.0'
```

2. Install a package that has `@types/react` in its `devDependencies` (e.g., `@sentry/react`):

```bash
pnpm add @sentry/react
```

3. Create a `.pnpmfile.cjs` to observe the plugin's input:

```javascript
const { hooks: pluginHooks } = require('./node_modules/.pnpm-config/@pnpm/plugin-types-fixer/pnpmfile.cjs')

module.exports = {
  hooks: {
    readPackage(pkg) {
      if (pkg.name === '@sentry/react') {
        console.log('devDependencies keys:', Object.keys(pkg.devDependencies).length)
      }
      return pluginHooks.readPackage(pkg)
    }
  }
}
```

4. Delete `node_modules` and `pnpm-lock.yaml`, then re-run `pnpm install`.

5. Observe that `devDependencies keys: 0` is logged — the plugin receives an empty `devDependencies` and does nothing.

6. Now clear the store and install again:

```bash
pnpm install --store-dir /tmp/pnpm-fresh-store
```

7. Observe that `devDependencies keys: 21` is logged — the plugin works correctly with a fresh store.

### Describe the Bug

`@pnpm/plugin-types-fixer` (recommended at [pnpm.io/typescript](https://pnpm.io/typescript)) relies on reading `pkg.devDependencies` in the `readPackage` hook to promote `@types/*` packages to `dependencies` or `peerDependencies`. However, when a package is already cached in the pnpm store, the `readPackage` hook receives a manifest derived from `bundledManifest`, which **does not include `devDependencies`**.

The root cause is in [`store/cafs/src/normalizeBundledManifest.ts`](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/blob/main/store/cafs/src/normalizeBundledManifest.ts) — `BUNDLED_MANIFEST_FIELDS`

When the package is already in the store, `bundledManifest` is used as the manifest source in [`pkg-manager/package-requester/src/packageRequester.ts`](https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/blob/dcd16c7b36cf95dc2abb9b09a81d66e87cd3fe97/pkg-manager/package-requester/src/packageRequester.ts#L282C5-L285C6):

This results in `pkg.devDependencies` being `{}` in the `readPackage` hook, making the plugin a no-op.

**This makes the plugin non-deterministic** — its behavior depends on the local store state:

| Store state | `pkg.devDependencies` in hook | Plugin effect |
|---|---|---|
| Not in store (fresh) | Populated from registry | ✅ Works |
| Already in store (cached) | `{}` (empty) | ❌ No-op |


### Expected Behavior

`readPackage` should receive the full dependency manifest (including `devDependencies`) consistently,
so hooks can safely rely on `pkg.devDependencies` when needed (especially pnpm-maintained hooks like plugin-types-fixer).

At minimum, behavior should match documentation: pkg should be the registry response or package.json content,
not a store-cached subset that drops devDependencies, when invoking `readPackage`.

### Which Node.js version are you using?

v22.14.0

### Which operating systems have you used?

- [x] macOS
- [ ] Windows
- [ ] Linux

### If your OS is a Linux based, which one it is? (Include the version if relevant)

_No response_
pnpm/pnpm#10698.title: @pnpm/plugin-types-fixer does not work when packages are already cached in the store (bundledManifest strips devDependencies)

```js
      const parsed = nameVerFromPkgSnapshot(depPath, pkgSnapshot)
      name = parsed.name
      version = parsed.version
    } else {
      pkgSnapshot = opts.wantedPackages[depPath]
      if (pkgSnapshot) {
        const parsed = nameVerFromPkgSnapshot(depPath, pkgSnapshot)
        name = parsed.name
        version = parsed.version
      } else {
        name = opts.alias
        version = opts.ref
      }
      isMissing = true
      isSkipped = opts.skipped.has(depPath)
    }
    resolved = (pkgSnapshotToResolution(depPath, pkgSnapshot, opts.registries) as TarballResolution).tarball
```
https://github.com/pnpm/pnpm/blob/main/reviewing/dependencies-hierarchy/src/getPkgInfo.ts#L71-L90

In pkgSnapshotToResolution:

```js
function pkgSnapshotToResolution(depPath, pkgSnapshot, registries) {
  // Accessing pkgSnapshot.resolution triggers the TypeError
  if (Boolean(pkgSnapshot.resolution.type) || ...) { 
    return pkgSnapshot.resolution;
  }
}
```

The command should gracefully handle missing snapshots (perhaps by skipping resolution or using a fallback) instead of crashing.

_No response_

_No response_

### Reproduction steps

1. Create a pnpm monorepo on Windows (NTFS filesystem)
2. 2. Add git worktrees: `git worktree add ./variant-a` + `git worktree add ./variant-b`
3. 3. Run `pnpm install` inside the worktrees — pnpm creates NTFS junctions in `node_modules` pointing to the main repo packages and global pnpm store
4. 4. Delete the worktree directory using PowerShell: `Remove-Item -Recurs1. Create a pnpm monorepo on Windows (NTFS filesystem)
5. 2. Add git worktrees: `git worktree add ./variant-a` + `git worktree add ./variant-b`
6. 3. Run `pnpm install` inside the worktrees — pnpm creates NTFS **junctions** in `node_modules` pointing to the main repo packages and the global pnpm store
7. 4. Delete the worktree directory using either:
8.    - PowerShell: `Remove-Item -Recurse -Force ./variant-a`
9.    - Git Bash/MSYS: `rm -rf ./variant-a`
10. 5. PowerShell/MSYS follows the NTFS junctions inside `node_modules` → deletes REAL target directories outside the worktree
11. 6. Catastrophic data loss — Windows user profile folders, source code, other projects permanently deleted. Recycle Bin bypassed.e -Force ./variant-a`
12.    OR using Git Bash/MSYS: `rm -rf ./variant-a`
13. 5. PowerShell/MSYS follows the NTFS junctions inside `node_modules` → deletes REAL target directories
14. 6. Catastrophic deletion outside the target directory — user profile folders, other projects, etc.

### Describe the Bug

**Real-world incident — February 26, 2026. Two users lost their entire Windows user profiles on the same day.**

pnpm uses NTFS **junctions** (directory reparse points) on Windows to link packages in `node_modules` to the global store and between workspaces. This is by design for performance, but creates a catastrophic danger: standard recursive deletion commands (`Remove-Item -Recurse -Force` in PowerShell, `rm -rf` in Git Bash/MSYS) follow these junctions into their real targets and delete files outside the intended directory.

**Actual data lost (my incident):**
- `C:\Users\wilbe\Documents\`, `Downloads\`, `Music\`, `Pictures\`, `Videos\`, `Favorites\` — ALL PERMANENTLY DELETED
- - Monorepo source code, 2 unpushed git commits, uncommitted work — GONE
- - - Windows Recycle Bin completely bypassed
**Second victim same day:** anthropics/claude-code#29023 — user msafakaraca lost their entire `C:\Users\msafa\` profile including a production SaaS application serving 50+ tax clients.

**This was triggered by Claude Code CLI**, which executed `Remove-Item -Recurse -Force` on git worktrees without knowing about this danger.

**Known PowerShell bugs:** PowerShell/PowerShell#16664, #19714 (both OPEN), git-for-windows/git#607
Deleting a pnpm worktree directory should only delete files within that directory. Junction points in `node_modules` should be removed as links, NOT followed into their real target directories. The behavior should be identical to `cmd.exe /c "rmdir /S /Q <path>"` which correctly handles this.
**Request for pnpm:**
1. Add a prominent **WARNING** in Windows documentation about this danger
2. 2. Document the safe deletion method: `cmd.exe /c "rmdir /S /Q <path>"`
3. 3. Consider adding a `pnpm worktree remove` or `pnpm clean` command that safely removes node_modules on Windows
4. 4. Consider using symlinks instead of junctions where possible (PowerShell handles symlinks correctly)
**Related issues:** PowerShell/PowerShell#26913, anthropics/claude-code#29249, #29023, #29082

### Expected Behavior

Deleting a directory containing pnpm `node_modules` should ONLY delete files within that directory. NTFS junction points should be removed as links (the junction itself), NOT followed into their real target directories. Safe method: `cmd.exe /c "rmdir /S /Q <path>"`
